([Yolngu](/wiki/Yol%C5%8Bu_Matha "Yolŋu Matha") for "child and mother", pronounced [/ˌjɒθuː ˈjɪndi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), natively [[ju:t̪u jindi]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an Australian musical group with [Aboriginal](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal "Australian Aboriginal") and non-Aboriginal members, formed in 1986 as a merger of two bands formed in 1985 – a white rock group called the **Swamp Jockeys** (Todd Williams, Michael Wyatt, Cal Williams, Stuart Kellaway, Andrew Bellety), and an unnamed Aboriginal folk group consisting of [Mandawuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu"), [Witiyana Marika](/wiki/Witiyana_Marika "Witiyana Marika"), and Milkayngu Mununggur. The Aboriginal members came from [Yolngu homelands](/wiki/Yolngu "Yolngu") near [Yirrkala](/wiki/Yirrkala,_Northern_Territory "Yirrkala, Northern Territory") on the [Gove Peninsula](/wiki/Gove_Peninsula "Gove Peninsula") in [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory")'s [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land"). Founding members included Stuart Kellaway on bass guitar, Cal Williams on lead guitar, Andrew Belletty on drums, Witiyana Marika on [manikay](/wiki/Manikay "Manikay") (traditional vocals), [bilma](/wiki/Bilma "Bilma") (ironwood [clapsticks](/wiki/Clapstick "Clapstick")) and dance, Milkayngu Mununggurr on [yidaki](/wiki/Yidaki "Yidaki"), [Geoffrey Gurrumul Yunupingu](/wiki/Geoffrey_Gurrumul_Yunupingu "Geoffrey Gurrumul Yunupingu") on keyboards, guitar, and percussion, past lead singer [Mandawuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu") and present Yirrnga Yunupingu on vocals and guitar.

**Yothu Yindi** ([Yolngu](/wiki/Yol%C5%8Bu_Matha "Yolŋu Matha") for "child and mother", pronounced [/ˌjɒθuː ˈjɪndi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), natively [[ju:t̪u jindi]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an Australian musical group with [Aboriginal](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal "Australian Aboriginal") and non-Aboriginal members, formed in 1986 as a merger of two bands formed in 1985 – a white rock group called the **Swamp Jockeys** (Todd Williams, Michael Wyatt, Cal Williams, Stuart Kellaway, Andrew Bellety), and an unnamed Aboriginal folk group consisting of [Mandawuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu"), [Witiyana Marika](/wiki/Witiyana_Marika "Witiyana Marika"), and Milkayngu Mununggur. The Aboriginal members came from [Yolngu homelands](/wiki/Yolngu "Yolngu") near [Yirrkala](/wiki/Yirrkala,_Northern_Territory "Yirrkala, Northern Territory") on the [Gove Peninsula](/wiki/Gove_Peninsula "Gove Peninsula") in [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory")'s [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land"). Founding members included Stuart Kellaway on bass guitar, Cal Williams on lead guitar, Andrew Belletty on drums, Witiyana Marika on [manikay](/wiki/Manikay "Manikay") (traditional vocals), [bilma](/wiki/Bilma "Bilma") (ironwood [clapsticks](/wiki/Clapstick "Clapstick")) and dance, Milkayngu Mununggurr on [yidaki](/wiki/Yidaki "Yidaki"), [Geoffrey Gurrumul Yunupingu](/wiki/Geoffrey_Gurrumul_Yunupingu "Geoffrey Gurrumul Yunupingu") on keyboards, guitar, and percussion, past lead singer [Mandawuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu") and present Yirrnga Yunupingu on vocals and guitar.

The band combines aspects of both musical cultures. Their sound varies from traditional Aboriginal songs to modern pop and rock songs, where they blended the typical instruments associated with pop/rock bands, such as guitars and drums, with the traditional [yidaki (didgeridoo](/wiki/Yidaki "Yidaki")) and [bilma (clap stick)](/wiki/Clapstick "Clapstick"). They adapted traditional Yolngu dance performances to accompany their music. More broadly, they promoted mutual respect and understanding in the coming together of different cultures.

Yothu Yindi's most widely known song, "[Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_(song) "Treaty (song)")", peaked at No. 11 on the [ARIA singles charts](/wiki/ARIA_Charts "ARIA Charts") in 1991 and the related album *[Tribal Voice](/wiki/Tribal_Voice "Tribal Voice")* peaked at No. 4 on the ARIA albums charts.

The group established the Yothu Yindi Foundation in 1990 to promote Yolngu cultural development, including from 1999 producing the annual [Garma Festival of Traditional Cultures](/wiki/Garma_Festival_of_Traditional_Cultures "Garma Festival of Traditional Cultures") and from May 2007 running the Dilthan Yolngunha (meaning "Healing Place"). Chairman of the foundation was [Galarrwuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Galarrwuy_Yunupingu "Galarrwuy Yunupingu"). He was Mandawuy's older brother, a Yolngu clan leader and sometimes a member of Yothu Yindi on bilma and guitar. Galarrwuy had been named [Australian of the Year](/wiki/Australian_of_the_Year "Australian of the Year") in 1978 for his work for Aboriginal communities and Mandawuy was [Australian of the Year](/wiki/Australian_of_the_Year "Australian of the Year") for 1992 for his work with Yothu Yindi. In December 2012, the [Australian Recording Industry Association](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") (ARIA) inducted the band into the [ARIA Hall of Fame](/wiki/ARIA_Hall_of_Fame "ARIA Hall of Fame"), as part of the [ARIA Music Awards of 2012](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_2012 "ARIA Music Awards of 2012"). In 2023, the band were inducted into the [National Indigenous Music Awards](/wiki/National_Indigenous_Music_Awards "National Indigenous Music Awards") Hall of Fame.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1986–1990: Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1986–1990: Early years")]

Swamp Jockeys were formed in 1985 by *balanda* (European/non-Aboriginal people) Todd Williams songwriter and lead singer, Michael Wyatt, songwriter and lead singer, Andrew Belletty on drums, Stuart Kellaway on bass guitar and Cal Williams on lead guitar.[[1]](#cite_note-ARDb-1) On their tour of [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land"), in Australia's [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), they were supported by a [Yolngu](/wiki/Yolngu "Yolngu") band composed of Witiyana Marika on *manikay* (traditional vocals), *bilma* (ironwood [clapsticks](/wiki/Clapstick "Clapstick")) and dance, Milkayngu Mununggurr on *yidaki* ([didgeridoo](/wiki/Didgeridoo "Didgeridoo")), [Gurrumul 'The Guru' Yunupingu](/wiki/Geoffrey_Gurrumul_Yunupingu "Geoffrey Gurrumul Yunupingu") on keyboards, guitar and percussion, and [Bakamana Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu") on vocals and guitar. They united to form Yothu Yindi (pronounced [/ˌjɒθuː ˈjɪndiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")).[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[1]](#cite_note-ARDb-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) *Yothu yindi* is a [Yolngu *matha*](/wiki/Yolngu_Matha "Yolngu Matha") (Yolngu language) kinship term for "child and mother".

The band combines aspects of both musical cultures. Their sound varies from traditional Aboriginal songs to modern pop and rock songs in which they blend the typical instruments of pop/rock bands, such as guitars and drums, with the traditional [yidaki](/wiki/Yidaki "Yidaki") and [bilma](/wiki/Bilma "Bilma"). They have adapted traditional Yolngu dance performances to accompany their music. More broadly they promote mutual respect and understanding of different cultures.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) Michael Wyatt, from the Swamp Jockeys, went on to become Yothu Yindi's pilot and with Stephen Johnson made Yothu Yindi's multiple award winning music video clips. He was also stage manager on Australian tours and their tour to the New York's World Music festival.

Bakamana Yunupingu was a tertiary student studying to become a teacher. He became principal at his own [Yirrkala](/wiki/Yirrkala,_Northern_Territory "Yirrkala, Northern Territory") Community School, and touring by Yothu Yindi was restricted to school holidays in the band's early years.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) In August 1988 they performed in [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville"), Queensland, at the South Pacific Festival of Arts. The next month they represented Australia in [Seoul, South Korea](/wiki/Seoul,_South_Korea "Seoul, South Korea") at the Cultural Olympics. [Bart Willoughby](/wiki/Bart_Willoughby "Bart Willoughby") (ex-[No Fixed Address](/wiki/No_Fixed_Address_(band) "No Fixed Address (band)"), [Coloured Stone](/wiki/Coloured_Stone "Coloured Stone")) joined on drums in late 1988 and Yothu Yindi toured USA and Canada as support act to [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil"). Upon their return to Australia, they were signed to [Mushroom Records](/wiki/Mushroom_Records "Mushroom Records"), and with Leszek Karski (ex-Supercharge) producing, recorded their debut single "Mainstream", released in March 1989. It was followed by debut album *[Homeland Movement](/wiki/Homeland_Movement_(album) "Homeland Movement (album)")* in May; a second single "[Djäpana (Sunset Dreaming)](/wiki/Dj%C3%A4pana "Djäpana")" was released in August.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[1]](#cite_note-ARDb-1) Neither their singles nor album had any major chart success.[[4]](#cite_note-AusCharts-4) Yothu Yindi toured with [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young") in Australia, then head-lined in Papua New Guinea and Hong Kong. In 1990 they toured New Zealand with [Tracy Chapman](/wiki/Tracy_Chapman "Tracy Chapman"), and then performed in festivals in the UK.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) In 1990 five clans of the Yolngu formed the Yothu Yindi Foundation to promote Yulngu cultural development.[[5]](#cite_note-YYF-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Heal-6) Chairman of the foundation was Galarrwuy Yunupingu,[[6]](#cite_note-Heal-6) Mandawuy's older brother, a Yolngu clan leader and sometimes a member of Yothu Yindi on *bilma* and guitar.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3)[[5]](#cite_note-YYF-5) Galarrwuy had been named [Australian of the Year](/wiki/Australian_of_the_Year "Australian of the Year") in 1978 for his work for Aboriginal communities.[[6]](#cite_note-Heal-6) Around this time, a relative of Bakamana who bore the same name died, and he therefore changed his first name to Mandawuy, in line with Yolngu tradition.

The band commissioned their friend and relative (married to Gurrumul's sister), master yiḏaki-maker [Djalu Gurruwiwi](/wiki/Djalu_Gurruwiwi "Djalu Gurruwiwi"), to make their didgeridoos, which also brought his skills to the attention of the world.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### 1991–1992: "Treaty" and *Tribal Voice*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1991–1992: \"Treaty\" and Tribal Voice")]

In 1988, as part of [Bicentennial](/wiki/Australian_Bicentenary#1988 "Australian Bicentenary") celebrations, Australian Prime Minister [Bob Hawke](/wiki/Bob_Hawke "Bob Hawke") visited the Northern Territory for the Barunga festival where he was presented with a statement of Aboriginal political objectives by Galarrwuy Yunupingu and [Wenten Rubuntja](/wiki/Wenten_Rubuntja "Wenten Rubuntja").[[8]](#cite_note-Barunga-8) Hawke responded to the Barunga Statement with a promise that a treaty would be concluded with [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") by 1990.[[8]](#cite_note-Barunga-8) By 1991, Yothu Yindi were Hughie Benjamin on drums, Sophie Garrkali and Julie Gungunbuy as dancers, Kellaway, Marika, Mununggurr, Gurrumul Yunupingu, Makuma Yunupingu on *yidaki*, vocals, *bilma*, Mandawuy Yunupingu, Mangatjay Yunupingu as a dancer.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2) Mandawuy, with his older brother Galarrwuy, wanted a song to highlight the lack of progress on the treaty between Aboriginal peoples and the federal government. Mandawuy recalls:

> Bob Hawke visited the Territory. He went to this gathering in Barunga. And this is where he made a statement that there shall be a treaty between black and white Australia. Sitting around the camp fire, trying to work out a chord to the guitar, and around that camp fire, I said, "Well, I heard it on the radio. And I saw it on the television." That should be a catchphrase. And that's where 'Treaty' was born.[[9]](#cite_note-GNT-9)

— Mandawuy Yunupingu, 8 July 2004

"Treaty" was written by Australian musician [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)") and Yothu Yindi members Mandawuy Yunupingu, Kellaway, Williams, Gurrumul Yunupingu, Mununggurr and Marika.[[10]](#cite_note-APRA2001-10)[[11]](#cite_note-ASCAP-11) The initial release had little interest,[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) but when Melbourne-based dance remixers [Filthy Lucre](/wiki/Filthy_Lucre_(music_producer) "Filthy Lucre (music producer)")'s [Gavin Campbell](/wiki/Gavin_Campbell_(music_producer) "Gavin Campbell (music producer)") and Robert Goodge adapted the song, their version peaked at No. 11 on the [Australian Recording Industry Association](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") (ARIA) singles [charts](/wiki/ARIA_Charts "ARIA Charts") by September.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[4]](#cite_note-AusCharts-4) The song contains lyrics in both English and in Yolngu *matha*. It was accompanied by a video showing band members performing vocals, music, and dance.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3)

Success for the single was transferred to the related album *[Tribal Voice](/wiki/Tribal_Voice "Tribal Voice")* which peaked at No. 4 on the ARIA albums charts,[[4]](#cite_note-AusCharts-4) The album, produced by Mark Moffatt for Mushroom Records, was released in September 1991.[[1]](#cite_note-ARDb-1) Mandawuy Yunupingu took leave of absence from his duties as principal to tour and promote the single and album.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) Other singles from the album were a re-released "[Djäpana (Sunset Dreaming)](/wiki/Dj%C3%A4pana "Djäpana")" which peaked at No. 13 in 1992 and "[Tribal Voice](/wiki/Tribal_Voice_(song) "Tribal Voice (song)")" which peaked at number 51.[[4]](#cite_note-AusCharts-4)

At the 1992 [ARIA Awards](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards") Yothu Yindi won awards for [ARIA Award for Best Cover Art](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Cover_Art "ARIA Award for Best Cover Art") for *Tribal Voice* by Louise Beach and Mushroom Art; [ARIA Award for Engineer of the Year](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Engineer_of_the_Year "ARIA Award for Engineer of the Year") for "Maralitja" (*maralitja* is Yolngu *matha* for crocodile man – one of Mandawuy's tribal names), "Dharpa" (*dharpa* is tree), "Treaty", "Treaty (Filthy Lucre remix)" and "Tribal Voice" by David Price, Ted Howard, Greg Henderson and Simon Polinski; [ARIA Award for Best Indigenous Release](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Indigenous_Release "ARIA Award for Best Indigenous Release") for *Tribal Voice*; [ARIA Award for Song of the Year](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "ARIA Award for Song of the Year") and Single of the Year for "Treaty.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-ARIAlist-13) Both "Treaty" in 1992 and "[Djäpana (Sunset Dreaming)](/wiki/Dj%C3%A4pana "Djäpana")" in 1993 charted on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* [Hot Dance Club Play](/wiki/Hot_Dance_Club_Play "Hot Dance Club Play") singles charts, with "Treaty" peaking at No. 6,[[14]](#cite_note-BillS-14) *Tribal Voice* peaked at No. 3 on the *Billboard* Top World Music Albums chart in 1992.[[15]](#cite_note-BillA-15)

In October 1992, then Prime Minister [Paul Keating](/wiki/Paul_Keating "Paul Keating")'s government awarded Yothu Yindi with a $30,000 grant.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[16]](#cite_note-Hayw-16) The money was used to travel to New York, where they performed at the United Nations for the launch of International Year for the World's Indigenous People.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)[[16]](#cite_note-Hayw-16) Mandawuy Yunupingu was named [Australian of the Year](/wiki/Australian_of_the_Year "Australian of the Year") by the Keating government on 26 January 1993.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2) His older brother, Galarrwuy had been named Australian of the Year in 1978 for his work for Aboriginal communities.[[6]](#cite_note-Heal-6)

In 2009 'Treaty' was added to the [National Film and Sound Archive](/wiki/National_Film_and_Sound_Archive "National Film and Sound Archive")'s Sounds of Australia Registry.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 1993–2000: Continued success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1993–2000: Continued success")]

At the 1993 ARIA Awards, Yothu Yindi won 'Best Video', Burrundi Pictures Michael Wyatt and Stephen Johnson, directed by [Stephen Maxwell Johnson](/wiki/Stephen_Maxwell_Johnson "Stephen Maxwell Johnson"), and 'Best Indigenous Release' for "[Djäpana (Sunset Dreaming)](/wiki/Dj%C3%A4pana "Djäpana")" and 'Engineer of the Year' for Greg Henderson's work on "Djäpana" and "Tribal Voice".[[13]](#cite_note-ARIAlist-13)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Yothu Yindi's third album *[Freedom](/wiki/Freedom_(Yothu_Yindi_album) "Freedom (Yothu Yindi album)")* was released in November 1993, the line-up included Mandawuy, Gurrumul, Makuna and Mangatjay Yunupingu, Marika, Williams, Kellaway, Benjamin and Munumggurr; and new members [Banula Marika](/wiki/Banula_Marika "Banula Marika") on vocals and dance, Bunimburr Marika on *yidaki*, Natalie Gillespie on vocals, Jodie Cockatoo Creed on vocals and clan leader Galarrwuy Yunupingu on *bilma* and vocals.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2) After intense touring in 1994, Williams left Yothu Yindi and was replaced by Colin Simpson on guitar, they added Ben Hakalitz (ex-[Not Drowning Waving](/wiki/Not_Drowning_Waving "Not Drowning Waving")) on drums and Baruka Tau-Matagu on keyboards. Gurrumul Yunupingu had left by 1995 to live full-time on [Elcho Island](/wiki/Elcho_Island "Elcho Island"), he later formed [Saltwater Band](/wiki/Saltwater_Band "Saltwater Band") to record three albums, and in 2008 released [his self-titled solo album](/wiki/Gurrumul "Gurrumul").[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) Yothu Yindi's fourth album *[Birrkuta](/wiki/Birrkuta_%E2%80%93_Wild_Honey "Birrkuta – Wild Honey")* (*birrkuta* means wild honey) was released in August 1996.[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2)

"[I Am Australian](/wiki/I_Am_Australian "I Am Australian")" is a popular song written in 1987 by [Dobe Newton](/wiki/Dobe_Newton "Dobe Newton") of [The Bushwackers](/wiki/The_Bushwackers_(band) "The Bushwackers (band)") and [Bruce Woodley](/wiki/Bruce_Woodley "Bruce Woodley") of [The Seekers](/wiki/The_Seekers "The Seekers").[[19]](#cite_note-APRA_IAmAustralian-19) It was released as a single in 1997 by trio [Judith Durham](/wiki/Judith_Durham "Judith Durham") of The Seekers, [Russell Hitchcock](/wiki/Russell_Hitchcock "Russell Hitchcock") from [Air Supply](/wiki/Air_Supply "Air Supply") and Yothu Yindi's [Mandawuy Yunupingu](/wiki/Mandawuy_Yunupingu "Mandawuy Yunupingu") by [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") Australia and it peaked at No. 17 on the ARIA Singles Charts in June.[[20]](#cite_note-IAmAustralianCharts-20)

Yothu Yindi's fifth album *[One Blood](/wiki/One_Blood_(Yothu_Yindi_album) "One Blood (Yothu Yindi album)")* was released in 1999 and included "Treaty '98".[[2]](#cite_note-McF-2) They sponsored the Yothu Yindi Foundation, which produces the annual [Garma Festival of Traditional Cultures](/wiki/Garma_Festival_of_Traditional_Cultures "Garma Festival of Traditional Cultures") celebrating Yolngu culture from 1999,[[5]](#cite_note-YYF-5) and their sixth album *[Garma](/wiki/Garma_(album) "Garma (album)")* was released in 2000,[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3) with Cal Williams returning on guitars.[[21]](#cite_note-Band-21) In 2000, Yothu Yindi performed at the closing ceremony of the [Sydney Olympic Games](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics").[[3]](#cite_note-Howl-3)

On 9 August 2000, 30-year-old Betsy Yunupingu was kicked in the head. She subsequently died, Yothu Yindi band member Gavin Makuma Yunupingu was found guilty of "committing a dangerous act causing death" and in June 2002 he was sentenced to 15 months imprisonment at [Berrimah Jail](/wiki/Berrimah_Jail "Berrimah Jail"), [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Jury-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Jailed-24) Gavin is the son of Galarrwuy and nephew of Mandawuy.[[23]](#cite_note-Jury-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Jailed-24)

### 2001–2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2001–2020")]

In May 2001 the [Australasian Performing Right Association (APRA)](/wiki/Australasian_Performing_Right_Association "Australasian Performing Right Association"), as part of its 75th-anniversary celebrations, named "Treaty" as one of the [Top 30 Australian songs](/wiki/APRA_Top_30_Australian_songs "APRA Top 30 Australian songs") of all time.[[10]](#cite_note-APRA2001-10)[[25]](#cite_note-Kruger-25) In 2003 Yothu Yindi toured through Northern Territory schools with Mandawuy Yunupingu, *yidaki* players Gapanbulu Yunupingu and Nicky Yunupingu, and Kellaway using songs, storytelling and open discussions to inspire and encourage some of Australia's most vulnerable young people to attend school and stay healthy. The Yothu Yindi Foundation in May 2007 established the Dilthan Yolngunha (Healing Place) using traditional healing practices and mainstream medicines.[[5]](#cite_note-YYF-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Heal-6) On 23 July 2008 a 23-year-old woman was stabbed numerous times; "yidaki" player N. Yunupingu, who was described by Northern Territory police as the offender, was later found dead by hanging.[[26]](#cite_note-NYunupingu-26) N. Yunupingu was the nephew of both Galarrwuy and Mandawuy Yunupingu, and, as members of Yothu Yindi, they had just played a concert for Prime Minister [Kevin Rudd](/wiki/Kevin_Rudd "Kevin Rudd") some hours before the stabbing of the woman, who was admitted to hospital, and N. Yunupingu's subsequent death.[[26]](#cite_note-NYunupingu-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 2009, News.com.au reported that Yothu Yindi lead singer Mandawuy Yunupingu needed a kidney transplant. Yunupingu said he drank up to four cartons of alcohol a day. "Alcohol was a big influence in my life. I didn't know what harm it did to my body. Before I knew, it was too late," he said.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

At the [ARIA Music Awards of 2012](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_2012 "ARIA Music Awards of 2012"), Yothu Yindi were inducted into the [ARIA Hall of Fame](/wiki/ARIA_Hall_of_Fame "ARIA Hall of Fame"), with [Peter Garrett](/wiki/Peter_Garrett "Peter Garrett") (then a former member of [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil")) and [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)") introducing the group.[[29]](#cite_note-ARIAHoF-29)[[30]](#cite_note-McCabe-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Middleton-31)

ARIA chairman and CEO of Sony Music Entertainment Australia and New Zealand, [Denis Handlin](/wiki/Denis_Handlin "Denis Handlin") said "On behalf of the ARIA Board it is with great honour that we induct Yothu Yindi into the ARIA Hall of Fame. Yothu Yindi created a special place in the Nation's heart through their passionate and ground breaking music. Their achievements remain a lasting heritage in both our community and overseas and we look forward to celebrating their induction at the ARIAs in is what is sure to be a magic moment".[[32]](#cite_note-32) The group were joined by Garrett, Kelly, [Jessica Mauboy](/wiki/Jessica_Mauboy "Jessica Mauboy") and [Dan Sultan](/wiki/Dan_Sultan "Dan Sultan") to perform "Treaty" at the ceremony.[[33]](#cite_note-Condon-33) In 2019 [Double J](/wiki/Double_J_(radio_station) "Double J (radio station)")"s Dan Condon described this as one of "7 great performances from the history of the ARIA Awards."[[33]](#cite_note-Condon-33)

The "best of" compilation, *[Healing Stone (The Best of Yothu Yindi)](/wiki/Healing_Stone_(The_Best_of_Yothu_Yindi) "Healing Stone (The Best of Yothu Yindi)")*, was released in November 2012 which included the new track "Healing Stone", produced by [Andrew Farriss](/wiki/Andrew_Farriss "Andrew Farriss") of INXS.

On 2 June 2013 lead singer M. Yunupingu died of [renal failure](/wiki/Renal_failure "Renal failure").[[34]](#cite_note-ABC_News-34) In line with Yolngu cultural protocols, on 4 June 2013 the family requested that the first names of the deceased no longer be used until further notice.[[35]](#cite_note-YothuYindi-35)

In 2015 they collaborated with rock-reggae band [East Journey](/wiki/East_Journey "East Journey") on a project called The Genesis Project, which included an [EP](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play")[[36]](#cite_note-36) and a performance at the [National Indigenous Music Awards 2015](/wiki/National_Indigenous_Music_Awards_2015 "National Indigenous Music Awards 2015").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

#### Yothu Yindi and The Treaty Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Yothu Yindi and The Treaty Project")]

In 2017, inspired by the 25th anniversary remix of "[Treaty (Filthy Lucre remix)](/wiki/Treaty_(Filthy_Lucre_remix) "Treaty (Filthy Lucre remix)")",[[38]](#cite_note-razor-38) some of the original members of Yothu Yindi, along with several new artists, created an [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica") project entitled Yothu Yindi & The Treaty Project (YYATTP[[39]](#cite_note-musicfeeds-39)). Longtime members Witiyana Marika, Malngay Yunupingu and Stuart Kellaway were joined by blues singer [Yirrmal](/wiki/Yirrmal "Yirrmal") (Marika's son), vocalists Yirrnga Yunupingu and Constantina Bush (aka Kamahi Djordon King), multi-instrumentalist Ania Reynolds (director of [Circus Oz](/wiki/Circus_Oz "Circus Oz")) and guitarist Megan Bernard,[[40]](#cite_note-themusic-40) while the [Filthy Lucre](/wiki/Filthy_Lucre_(music_producer) "Filthy Lucre (music producer)") duo Nick Coleman and [Gavin Campbell](/wiki/Gavin_Campbell_(music_producer) "Gavin Campbell (music producer)") worked on [mixing](/wiki/Mix_(music) "Mix (music)") and production.[[41]](#cite_note-mabo2019-41)

The group played on [ABC Radio's *The Friday Revue*](/wiki/ABC_Radio_Melbourne "ABC Radio Melbourne") and at the [Homeground](/wiki/Homeground "Homeground") festival at the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House"), which celebrates Indigenous culture (November 2017[[42]](#cite_note-42)), and created a [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") version of *Treaty* with [Baker Boy](/wiki/Baker_Boy "Baker Boy"). Inn January 2018 they were booked to play at the [Enmore Theatre](/wiki/Enmore_Theatre "Enmore Theatre") with [The Herd](/wiki/The_Herd_(Australian_band) "The Herd (Australian band)").[[40]](#cite_note-themusic-40) They also played [Strawberry Fields Festival](/wiki/Strawberry_Fields_Festival "Strawberry Fields Festival"), [Queenscliff Music Festival](/wiki/Queenscliff_Music_Festival "Queenscliff Music Festival") at the opening night of the [Gold Coast 2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Gold_Coast_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Gold Coast 2018 Commonwealth Games"),[[38]](#cite_note-razor-38) continuing live performances into at least March 2019, including a tour to New Zealand,[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[39]](#cite_note-musicfeeds-39) playing on [Waitangi Day](/wiki/Waitangi_Day "Waitangi Day") 2019 in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").[[41]](#cite_note-mabo2019-41)

The group have been described as a "fluid collective", with other musicians quite often joining them on stage. [Shane Howard](/wiki/Shane_Howard "Shane Howard"), frontman of the band [Goanna](/wiki/Goanna_(band) "Goanna (band)"), has regularly played with them.[[39]](#cite_note-musicfeeds-39) They released the Yothu Yindi song "Mabo" for the first time as a single in April 2019.[[41]](#cite_note-mabo2019-41)

### 2021–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2021–present")]

On 26 June 2021, Yothu Yindi played at the [Yarrapay Festival](/wiki/Yarrapay_Festival "Yarrapay Festival"), which was directed by Witiyana Marika, at [Buku-Larrnggay Mulka Art Centre](/wiki/Buku-Larrnggay_Mulka_Centre "Buku-Larrnggay Mulka Centre") in [Yirrkala](/wiki/Yirrkala "Yirrkala"), along with the [Andrew Gurruwiwi Band](/wiki/Andrew_Gurruwiwi "Andrew Gurruwiwi"), Yirrmal, and [East Journey](/wiki/East_Journey "East Journey").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

In August 2023, Yothu Yindi were inducted into the NIMAs ([National Indigenous Music Awards](/wiki/National_Indigenous_Music_Awards_2023 "National Indigenous Music Awards 2023")) Hall of Fame for their trailblazing contribution to Indigenous music and the rights of First Nations people.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-rangiah2023-46)

## Collaborations and impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Collaborations and impact")]

Many other musicians apart from Paul Kelly have collaborated or performed with Yothu Yindi, including [Jimmy Barnes](/wiki/Jimmy_Barnes "Jimmy Barnes"), [Billy Thorpe](/wiki/Billy_Thorpe "Billy Thorpe"), members of the [Grateful Dead](/wiki/Grateful_Dead "Grateful Dead"),[[46]](#cite_note-rangiah2023-46) [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil"), [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young"), [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) and [Emma Donovan](/wiki/Emma_Donovan "Emma Donovan").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Dhapanbal Yunupiŋu, daughter of Mandawuy Yunupiŋu, is a singer-songwriter, who credits the band with providing inspiration to many young Yolngu musicians. Among these are the band [King Stingray](/wiki/King_Stingray "King Stingray"), whose line-up includes Mandawuy's nephew, Yirrŋa Yunupingu, as lead vocalist, and Stuart Kellaway's son Roy Kellaway on guitar.[[46]](#cite_note-rangiah2023-46)

### Mo Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yothu_Yindi&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Mo Awards")]

The Australian Entertainment Mo Awards (commonly known informally as the [Mo Awards](/wiki/Mo_Awards "Mo Awards")), were annual Australian entertainment industry awards. They recognise achievements in live entertainment in Australia from 1975 to 2016. Yothu Yindi won three awards in that time.[[71]](#cite_note-71)
